title: Types of Bank Accounts
content:
Types of Bank Accounts

1. Checking Accounts
- Purpose: For frequent, everyday transactions
- Features: Unlimited deposits and withdrawals, debit card access, check-writing
- Interest: Typically earns little to no interest
- Fees: May have monthly maintenance fees or per-check fees
- Best for: People who frequently deposit and withdraw money
- FDIC Coverage: Yes, up to $250,000

2. Savings Accounts
- Purpose: For storing money and earning interest
- Features: Limited number of withdrawals per month, competitive interest rates
- Interest: Earns interest on balance, rates vary by bank and market
- Fees: May have monthly maintenance fees for low balance
- Best for: Building emergency fund or saving for future goals
- FDIC Coverage: Yes, up to $250,000

3. Money Market Accounts
- Purpose: Hybrid between checking and savings
- Features: Check-writing privileges, higher interest rates
- Interest: Higher rates than regular savings, tied to market conditions
- Fees: May require higher minimum balance, monthly maintenance fees
- Best for: People wanting both access and competitive returns
- FDIC Coverage: Yes, up to $250,000

4. Credit Cards
- Purpose: Borrow money for purchases, pay back over time
- Features: Rewards programs, cash back, travel benefits
- Interest: Interest charged on unpaid balance (APR varies)
- Fees: Annual fees (varies), late payment fees, foreign transaction fees
- Best for: Building credit history and managing short-term purchases
- FDIC Coverage: Not FDIC insured (credit product, not deposit account)

title: How to Open a Bank Account
content:
How to Open a Bank Account

Step-by-Step Process:

1. Required Documents
- Government-issued photo ID (driver's license, passport, national ID)
- Social Security Number (SSN) or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN)
- Proof of address (utility bill, lease agreement, or recent bank statement dated within 90 days)
- Initial deposit (amount varies by account type, typically $25-100)

2. Account Opening Methods
- In-person: Visit a branch with documents, takes 15-30 minutes
- Online: Upload documents, verify identity digitally, takes 10-20 minutes
- Phone: Call customer service, provide information verbally, takes 20-30 minutes

3. Identity Verification
- May require video call for online applications
- Must verify address (may send verification letter)
- Security questions to confirm identity

4. Approval Timeline
- Instant: Most online accounts approved immediately
- 24 hours: Some applications require manual review
- Up to 5 business days: Special cases or suspected fraud

5. Account Features Setup
- Debit card ordering (arrives in 7-10 business days)
- Online banking activation
- Mobile app setup
- Initial checks (if checking account)

6. First Deposit
- Can transfer from another bank (ACH, takes 1-2 days)
- Can deposit check via mobile app (clearing takes 2-5 days)
- Can set up direct deposit (setup takes 1-2 business days to activate)

title: Banking Fees and Charges
content:
Banking Fees and Charges

Account Maintenance Fees:
- Monthly maintenance: $5-$15 per month (waived if minimum balance maintained)
- Inactivity fee: $5-$25 if no transactions for 6-12 months
- Minimum balance fee: $5-$10 if balance falls below required minimum

Transaction Fees:
- Overdraft fee: $25-$35 per transaction when account balance insufficient
- Non-sufficient funds (NSF) fee: $25-$35 if payment rejected due to low balance
- Wire transfer fee: $15-$25 per wire (domestic or international)
- ATM out-of-network fee: $2-$4 per withdrawal at ATM not in our network
- Foreign transaction fee: 1-3% of transaction amount for international purchases

Specialty Fees:
- Stop payment fee: $15-$25 per stop payment request
- Account closure fee: $25 if account closed within 90 days of opening
- Paper statement fee: $1-$3 per month if you request printed statements
- Duplicate statement fee: $5-$10 per statement copy

Credit Card Fees:
- Annual fee: $0-$500+ depending on card tier
- Late payment fee: $25-$35 if payment is 30+ days late
- Cash advance fee: 3-5% of amount or minimum $5-$10
- Balance transfer fee: 3-5% of amount or minimum $5-$10

Fee Waiver Programs:
- Direct deposit waives monthly maintenance fee
- Maintaining minimum balance ($1,000-$10,000) waives fees
- Student accounts: Reduced fees or fee-free for 6 years
- Senior accounts: Reduced fees if 65+ years old

title: FDIC Insurance and Account Safety
content:
FDIC Insurance and Account Safety

What is FDIC Insurance?
- FDIC (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation) is a government agency
- Protects deposits at member banks in case of bank failure
- Not against theft or fraud (this is covered by other protections)
- Covers most account types at member banks

Coverage Limits:
- Standard: $250,000 per depositor, per institution
- Joint accounts: $250,000 per person ($500,000 total if held with spouse)
- Retirement accounts (IRA): $250,000 per depositor, separate from regular accounts
- Trust accounts: $250,000 per unique beneficiary
- Employer Sponsored Retirement: $250,000 per account

What is NOT Covered:
- Credit card balances (credit products, not deposits)
- Stocks, bonds, mutual funds held at the bank
- Safe deposit box contents
- Money not yet confirmed deposited
- Deposits in foreign banks

How to Maximize Coverage:
- Spread money across multiple institutions (each covered separately)
- Use different account types (regular vs retirement vs trust)
- Add spouse to joint account (increases coverage to $500,000)
- Use Certificates of Deposit (CDs) for longer-term coverage

Recent Events and Confidence:
- FDIC insurance has been in effect since 1933
- No depositor has lost FDIC-insured money since inception
- Coverage increased from $100,000 to $250,000 in 2008 (emergency measure)
- Made permanent in 2010

How Deposits are Protected:
1. Bank failure detected
2. FDIC takes control of assets
3. Deposits within coverage limit are protected
4. Funds typically available within 1-5 business days
5. No action required from depositor

title: Overdraft Protection and NSF Prevention
content:
Overdraft Protection and NSF Prevention

What is Overdraft Protection?
- Service that prevents debit card/check transactions from being declined
- Connects to linked account (savings or credit line) to cover shortfalls
- Helps avoid embarrassing declined transactions
- Typically comes with fees

How It Works:
1. You attempt transaction exceeding available balance
2. System checks if overdraft protection is enabled
3. If enabled: Amount transferred from linked account, fee charged ($25-$35)
4. If disabled: Transaction declined, NSF fee charged ($25-$35)

Types of Overdraft Protection:
- Link to savings account: Uses savings balance to cover shortage
- Link to credit line: Draws from credit line at card rate
- Overdraft line of credit: Separate overdraft credit line
- Standing transfers: Automatic transfer from savings if balance low

Costs:
- Per-transaction fee: $25-$35 (not per dollar, per transaction)
- Monthly cap: Some banks limit to 3-6 overdrafts/month
- Interest on borrowed amount: If credit line used, interest charged

Advantages:
- Prevents declined transactions
- Protects checks from bouncing (expensive)
- More convenient than separate transactions
- Can save from cascading NSF fees

Disadvantages:
- Fees add up quickly ($25-35 per transaction)
- May encourage overspending if balance unknown
- Interest charges if credit line used
- Can lead to negative spiral of fees

Alternatives:
- Set up automatic transfers from savings for specific dates
- Use alerts/notifications when balance gets low
- Maintain minimum balance buffer
- Pay attention to pending transactions before spending

title: Account Security and Fraud Protection
content:
Account Security and Fraud Protection

Fraud Protection Features:
- Zero liability for unauthorized debit card transactions
- Zero liability for unauthorized credit card transactions
- 60-day window to report fraud and dispute unauthorized charges
- Temporary credit while investigation pending

Types of Fraud:
1. Debit Card Fraud: Card skimmed or information stolen
   - Report within 60 days for full protection
   - Protected while investigating

2. Credit Card Fraud: Unauthorized charges
   - Never liable for fraudulent charges (federal law)
   - May have $50 liability if reported after 60 days

3. Account Takeover: Hacker gains access to online banking
   - Unauthorized transfers may not be covered
   - Report immediately to minimize liability
   - Act quickly to change passwords and notify bank

4. Check Fraud: Forged checks or stolen checkbook
   - Report quickly if checkbook stolen
   - Liability depends on discovery time

Security Best Practices:
- Use strong passwords (mix of upper/lower/numbers/symbols)
- Never share passwords or security codes
- Use multi-factor authentication when available
- Monitor statements monthly for unauthorized activity
- Use secure networks (avoid public WiFi for banking)
- Update devices with latest security patches
- Shred documents with personal financial information
- Don't respond to phishing emails or texts

Monitoring Your Account:
- Review statements monthly (printed or online)
- Set up account alerts for large transactions
- Use credit monitoring services (often free for cardholders)
- Check credit report annually (free at annualcreditreport.com)
- Report suspicious activity immediately

If Fraud Occurs:
1. Contact bank immediately by phone (not email/text)
2. Report specific unauthorized transactions
3. Ask for temporary card or new account number
4. File dispute for each fraudulent transaction
5. Freeze credit with credit bureaus if needed
6. Monitor account closely for 30-60 days

title: Personal Loans and Borrowing Options
content:
Personal Loans and Borrowing Options

Personal Loans (Unsecured):
- Amount: $500-$100,000 typically
- Term: 2-7 years for repayment
- Interest rate: 5-36% APR based on credit score
- Use: Consolidate debt, home improvement, emergency expenses
- Requirements: Credit check, income verification
- Approval: 1-3 business days typically

Home Equity Loans:
- Amount: Up to 80-90% of home equity
- Term: 5-30 years for repayment
- Interest rate: Lower than personal loans (typically 5-12%)
- Use: Large projects, debt consolidation
- Risk: Your home is collateral; default means foreclosure
- Tax benefit: Interest may be tax-deductible

Auto Loans:
- Amount: Up to 100-110% of vehicle value
- Term: 3-7 years for repayment
- Interest rate: 3-11% APR based on credit
- Collateral: The vehicle itself
- Requirements: Down payment typically $0-20%
- Insurance: Required to maintain full coverage

Student Loans:
- Federal loans: 4-8% fixed rate, flexible repayment
- Private loans: 5-14% rates, credit-based
- Forgiveness: Some federal loan programs include forgiveness
- Income-driven repayment: Available for federal loans

Credit Cards:
- Revolving credit line
- Minimum monthly payment
- Interest charged on unpaid balance (typical APR: 15-25%)
- Available credit: Typically based on credit score and income

Factors Affecting Loan Approval:
- Credit score: 620+ for basic approval, 750+ for best rates
- Debt-to-income ratio: Should be below 43%
- Employment history: Stable employment preferred
- Existing debts: More debts may reduce approval chance
- Income verification: Proof of stable income required

title: Interest Rates and Returns
content:
Interest Rates and Returns

Understanding APR vs APY:
- APR (Annual Percentage Rate): Simple interest rate, doesn't include compounding
- APY (Annual Percentage Yield): Includes compounding effect, actual return on investment
- APY is always higher than or equal to APR due to compounding
- Example: 4% APR becomes ~4.06% APY with monthly compounding

Savings Account Interest:
- Current environment: 0.1-4.0% APY (varies by market and institution)
- Compounding: Usually daily or monthly
- Minimum balance: Often required to earn advertised rate
- FDIC insured: Yes, up to $250,000
- Tax implications: Interest taxed as ordinary income

Money Market Account Interest:
- Rates: Typically 0.1-4.5% APY (often higher than savings)
- Compounding: Usually daily
- Check writing: Limited checks per month (often 3 or 6)
- Minimum balance: Often $2,500-$10,000 to earn full rate
- Tiered rates: Higher rates for larger balances

Certificate of Deposit (CD) Interest:
- Terms: 3 months to 5+ years available
- Rates: Higher rates for longer terms (e.g., 1-year: 4%, 5-year: 4.5%)
- Fixed rate: Locked in for term of CD
- Early withdrawal penalty: 3-12 months of interest typically
- FDIC insured: Yes, up to $250,000

Credit Card Interest:
- Purchase APR: 15-25% typical (20% average)
- Cash advance APR: Often 5-10% higher than purchase rate
- Promotional rates: 0% APR for 6-21 months (balance transfer or purchase)
- Penalty APR: 27-30% if payment 60+ days late
- Prime rate driven: Corporate rates affect credit card APR

Mortgage Interest:
- 30-year fixed: Currently 4-7% depending on market
- 15-year fixed: Typically 0.5-1% lower than 30-year
- 5/1 ARM: Adjustable after 5 years, initial rate 3-5%
- Points/Fees: Can lower rate by 0.25% per point paid upfront

Factors Affecting Interest Rates:
- Federal Reserve rate: Base rate that affects all other rates
- Your credit score: 750+ gets best rates, below 650 gets penalty rates
- Economic conditions: Inflation and market conditions affect rates
- Term length: Longer terms typically have higher rates
- Loan type: Secured loans (mortgages) have lower rates than unsecured
